1:
"But there were false prophets also among the people, even as there shall be false teachers among you, who privily shall bring in damnable heresies, even denying the Lord that bought them, and bring upon themselves swift destruction." The witnesses note the warning of false teachers who deny the Lord that bought them — the atonement crux. On "there were false prophets also among the people, even as there shall be false teachers among you," the grammatical voices note the parallel. Barnes and Robertson note the historical comparison: "there were false prophets also among the people (Israel), even as there shall be false teachers among you" — as Israel had false prophets, so the church will have false teachers; the certainty of their rising.

On "who privily shall bring in damnable heresies," the witnesses note the secret error. Barnes and Calvin note that these "privily (secretly, stealthily) bring in damnable (destructive, leading to ruin) heresies (factious false doctrines)" — they smuggle in destructive errors, introducing ruinous false teaching covertly.

On "even denying the Lord that bought them," interpreters divide over the atonement. The wesleyan-arminian Wesley and Clarke, with the grammatical-historical Ellicott and Barnes, take "the Lord that bought (purchased, redeemed) them" to mean that Christ died even for these false teachers who perish — a proof that Christ's redemption extends to all, even apostates (Wesley: "Christ bought even them that perish"; Ellicott: "conclusive against Calvinistic doctrines of partial redemption"). The Reformed Gill (and the Calvinist tradition) hold that "bought" refers to their profession or to a purchase from temporal ruin/bondage — they were professedly redeemed, or the language describes them as the false teachers claimed, not that they were truly and effectually redeemed (since the truly redeemed cannot finally perish); Robertson notes "these were professing Christians." The verse displays the warning of false teachers who deny the Lord that bought them.

The witnesses agree the substance: false teachers will arise within the church, secretly introducing destructive heresies, denying the Lord, and bringing swift destruction on themselves (Barnes, Robertson, Calvin, Wesley). But "the Lord that bought them" divides them: the Arminian and broader reading takes it as proof Christ bought even those who perish (universal atonement — Wesley, Ellicott, Barnes); the Reformed reading takes "bought" as professed or temporal purchase, not effectual redemption (limited atonement — Gill). The verse displays the atonement-extent crux. The verse marks the false teachers: arising. The verse displays the heresies: damnable, secret. The verse displays the debate: Christ bought all, or professed purchase. The verse displays the destruction: swift, self-brought. The warning displays the false teachers denying their Lord. The verse displays the prophets: false. The verse displays the Lord: bought them. The verse displays the destruction: swift. The warning of false teachers who deny the Lord that bought them displays the opening of the chapter and a famous crux: false teachers will secretly bring in destructive heresies, denying the Lord that bought them — read as proof Christ redeemed even those who perish (Wesley, Ellicott, Barnes) or as a professed/temporal purchase short of effectual redemption (Gill) — bringing swift destruction on themselves. The verse displays the doomed false teachers, whose ways many follow (v.2).
2:
"And many shall follow their pernicious ways; by reason of whom the way of truth shall be evil spoken of." The brief verse notes that many follow the false teachers, bringing the truth into disrepute. The grammatical voices note the wide influence. Barnes and Robertson note the success of the error: "many shall follow their pernicious ways (their licentious conduct, their destructive courses)" — the false teachers will not lack followers; many will be drawn into their corrupt and ruinous ways.

On "their pernicious ways," the witnesses note the licentiousness. Barnes and Calvin note that the "pernicious ways" (some texts read "lasciviousness") point to the immoral, sensual conduct of the false teachers — their teaching issues in loose living, which many imitate.

On "the way of truth shall be evil spoken of," the puritan Henry notes the dishonour to the gospel: "by reason of whom the way of truth shall be evil spoken of (blasphemed, slandered)" — because these corrupt teachers and their followers bear the Christian name, the way of truth (the gospel) is brought into disrepute; outsiders blaspheme Christianity because of the scandalous lives of these professors. The verse displays that many follow the false teachers, bringing the truth into disrepute.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the influence — "many shall follow their pernicious ways (destructive, licentious courses)," the false teachers drawing many into corrupt ways (Barnes, Robertson); the "pernicious ways" pointing to immoral, sensual conduct that many imitate (Barnes, Calvin); "the way of truth shall be evil spoken of," the gospel blasphemed because of the scandalous lives of these professors (Henry). The verse displays the corrupting influence. The verse marks the followers: many. The verse displays the ways: pernicious. The verse displays the consequence: truth blasphemed. The verse displays the cause: scandalous lives. The influence displays the truth dishonoured by the false teachers' followers. The verse displays the many: following. The verse displays the ways: licentious. The verse displays the truth: slandered. That many follow the false teachers, bringing the truth into disrepute, displays the spreading harm of their error: many will follow their licentious ways, and through them the way of truth itself will be blasphemed by those who see the scandal. The verse displays the dishonoured truth, whose corrupters face sure judgment (v.3).
3:
"And through covetousness shall they with feigned words make merchandise of you: whose judgment now of a long time lingereth not, and their damnation slumbereth not." The witnesses note the covetous false teachers whose certain judgment does not slumber. On "through covetousness shall they with feigned words make merchandise of you," the grammatical voices note the greed. Barnes and Robertson note the motive: "through covetousness (greed for gain) shall they with feigned (fabricated, counterfeit) words make merchandise of (exploit, trade upon) you" — the false teachers are driven by greed, using deceptive words to exploit believers for profit; they treat the flock as merchandise to be sold for gain.

On "feigned words," the witnesses note the deceit. Barnes and Calvin note that their "feigned words" are plausible, manufactured arguments and false promises by which they deceive and fleece their hearers; the cover of greed with fair speech.

On "whose judgment now of a long time lingereth not, and their damnation slumbereth not," the puritan Henry notes the certain doom: "whose judgment now of a long time lingereth not (does not delay), and their damnation (destruction) slumbereth not (is not asleep)" — though long ago appointed, their judgment is not idle or delayed; their destruction is awake and ready, certain and impending, though it may seem to tarry. The verse displays the covetous false teachers whose certain judgment does not slumber.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the greed — "through covetousness shall they with feigned (counterfeit) words make merchandise of (exploit) you," the false teachers driven by greed, exploiting believers with deceptive words (Barnes, Robertson); "feigned words," plausible manufactured arguments to fleece the hearers (Barnes, Calvin); "whose judgment... lingereth not, and their damnation slumbereth not," their long-appointed judgment certain and awake, not delayed (Henry). The verse displays the doomed greed. The verse marks the motive: covetousness. The verse displays the means: feigned words. The verse displays the exploitation: merchandise. The verse displays the doom: not slumbering. The covetousness displays the false teachers exploiting the flock under certain judgment. The verse displays the covetousness: their drive. The verse displays the merchandise: the flock. The verse displays the judgment: awake. The covetous false teachers whose certain judgment does not slumber display their greed and their doom: driven by covetousness, they exploit believers with counterfeit words — but their long-appointed judgment does not linger, and their destruction is not asleep. The verse displays the impending judgment, proved certain by God's past judgments (vv.4-9).
4:
"For if God spared not the angels that sinned, but cast them down to hell, and delivered them into chains of darkness, to be reserved unto judgment;" The witnesses note God not sparing the sinning angels, reserved in chains for judgment. On "if God spared not the angels that sinned," the grammatical voices note the first example. Barnes and Robertson note the opening of the proof that judgment is sure: "if God spared not (did not exempt from punishment) the angels that sinned" — the first of three examples (angels, the flood, Sodom) proving God's certain judgment on the wicked; even angels, exalted as they were, were not spared when they sinned.

On "cast them down to hell, and delivered them into chains of darkness," the witnesses note the doom of the angels. Barnes and Calvin note the punishment: "cast them down to hell (tartarosas — consigned them to Tartarus, the abyss), and delivered them into chains (pits) of darkness" — the sinning angels were cast into the gloomy abyss, bound in darkness; their fall and confinement.

On "to be reserved unto judgment," the grammatical-historical note the reservation: they are "reserved (kept, held) unto judgment" — held in confinement awaiting the final judgment; their present confinement is not their final doom but a custody until the great day. The verse displays God not sparing the sinning angels, reserved for judgment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the first example — "if God spared not the angels that sinned," even exalted angels not spared, the first proof of certain judgment (Barnes, Robertson); "cast them down to hell (Tartarus), and delivered them into chains of darkness," the sinning angels cast into the gloomy abyss, bound in darkness (Barnes, Calvin); "to be reserved unto judgment," held in custody awaiting the final judgment (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the doomed angels. The verse marks the sparing: not even angels. The verse displays the casting: to hell. The verse displays the chains: of darkness. The verse displays the reservation: unto judgment. The example displays God judging even the angels who sinned. The verse displays the angels: fallen. The verse displays the chains: binding. The verse displays the judgment: reserved. God not sparing the sinning angels, reserved in chains for judgment, displays the first proof that the false teachers' judgment is sure: if God did not spare even the angels who sinned, but cast them into the abyss in chains of darkness to await judgment, the wicked will not escape. The verse displays the reserved angels, paralleled by the flood and Sodom (vv.5-6).
5:
"And spared not the old world, but saved Noah the eighth person, a preacher of righteousness, bringing in the flood upon the world of the ungodly;" The witnesses note God sparing not the old world but saving Noah in the flood. On "spared not the old world, but saved Noah the eighth person," the grammatical voices note the second example. Barnes and Robertson note the flood as proof: "spared not the old world (the antediluvian world), but saved Noah the eighth (person) (Noah with seven others, eight in all)" — God did not spare the wicked ancient world, yet preserved Noah and his family; judgment on the many, salvation for the few.

On "a preacher of righteousness," the witnesses note Noah's witness. Barnes and Calvin note that Noah was "a preacher (herald) of righteousness" — he proclaimed righteousness to the doomed world, warning them while the ark was preparing; the righteous witness amid the ungodly.

On "bringing in the flood upon the world of the ungodly," the grammatical-historical note the judgment: God brought "the flood (the deluge) upon the world of the ungodly" — the ungodly world was destroyed by the flood; God's judgment fell on the wicked, sparing only the righteous. The verse displays God sparing not the old world but saving Noah in the flood.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the second example — "spared not the old world, but saved Noah the eighth person," the wicked ancient world unspared, Noah and seven others preserved (Barnes, Robertson); "a preacher of righteousness," Noah proclaiming righteousness to the doomed (Barnes, Calvin); "bringing in the flood upon the world of the ungodly," the ungodly destroyed by the deluge (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the flood judgment. The verse marks the sparing: not the old world. The verse displays the saving: Noah the eighth. The verse displays the preacher: of righteousness. The verse displays the flood: on the ungodly. The example displays God judging the world but saving the righteous few. The verse displays the world: unspared. The verse displays the Noah: saved. The verse displays the flood: brought. God sparing not the old world but saving Noah in the flood displays the second proof of certain judgment: God did not spare the ancient world but brought the flood upon the ungodly — yet saved Noah, the preacher of righteousness, with seven others; judgment on the wicked, deliverance for the righteous. The verse displays the flood judgment, joined to the doom of Sodom (v.6).
6:
"And turning the cities of Sodom and Gomorrha into ashes condemned them with an overthrow, making them an ensample unto those that after should live ungodly;" The brief verse notes Sodom and Gomorrah made an example of judgment. The grammatical voices note the third example. Barnes and Robertson note the doom of the cities: "turning the cities of Sodom and Gomorrha into ashes condemned them with an overthrow (katastrophe — utter destruction)" — God reduced Sodom and Gomorrah to ashes, condemning them by total overthrow; the third proof of certain judgment on the ungodly.

On "condemned them with an overthrow," the witnesses note the total destruction. Barnes and Calvin note that the cities were utterly overthrown — destroyed by fire from heaven, reduced to ashes; the completeness of the judgment on their gross wickedness.

On "making them an ensample unto those that after should live ungodly," the puritan Henry notes the warning: God made them "an ensample (example, warning) unto those that after should live ungodly" — the destruction of Sodom stands as a perpetual example and warning to all future ungodly people; their doom is a sign of what awaits the wicked. The verse displays Sodom and Gomorrah made an example of judgment.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the third example — "turning the cities of Sodom and Gomorrha into ashes condemned them with an overthrow (utter destruction)," the cities reduced to ashes by total overthrow (Barnes, Robertson); the cities utterly destroyed by fire from heaven for their wickedness (Barnes, Calvin); "an ensample unto those that after should live ungodly," their doom a perpetual warning to the wicked (Henry). The verse displays the example of Sodom. The verse marks the cities: turned to ashes. The verse displays the condemnation: an overthrow. The verse displays the example: to the ungodly. The verse displays the warning: perpetual. The example displays Sodom's doom warning all future wicked. The verse displays the cities: destroyed. The verse displays the overthrow: total. The verse displays the ensample: set. Sodom and Gomorrah made an example of judgment display the third proof of certain doom: God turned the cities to ashes and condemned them with utter overthrow, making them a perpetual example and warning to all who would afterward live ungodly. The verse displays the warning example, from which the righteous Lot was delivered (vv.7-9).
7:
"And delivered just Lot, vexed with the filthy conversation of the wicked:" The brief verse notes the deliverance of righteous Lot, distressed by the wicked. The grammatical voices note the rescue of the righteous. Barnes and Robertson note the counterpart to the judgment: "delivered just (righteous) Lot, vexed (distressed, worn down) with the filthy conversation (the licentious conduct, behaviour) of the wicked" — amid the destruction of Sodom, God rescued righteous Lot, who had been grieved and distressed by the immoral conduct of the people around him.

On "just Lot," the witnesses note his righteousness. Barnes and Henry note that Lot is called "just (righteous)" — though he dwelt in Sodom, he was a righteous man; his deliverance shows that God knows how to rescue the godly even from the midst of the wicked.

On "vexed with the filthy conversation of the wicked," the puritan Henry notes his distress: Lot was "vexed (tormented, worn down)" by the "filthy conversation (lewd conduct) of the wicked" — the daily sight of their lawless immorality grieved his righteous soul; the godly are pained, not pleased, by surrounding wickedness. The verse displays the deliverance of righteous Lot, distressed by the wicked.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the rescue — "delivered just (righteous) Lot, vexed (distressed) with the filthy conversation (licentious conduct) of the wicked," God rescuing righteous Lot, grieved by the immorality around him (Barnes, Robertson); "just Lot," a righteous man though dwelling in Sodom, rescued from the wicked (Barnes, Henry); "vexed with the filthy conversation," the lewd conduct grieving his righteous soul (Henry). The verse displays the rescued righteous. The verse marks the deliverance: of Lot. The verse displays the righteousness: just Lot. The verse displays the distress: vexed. The verse displays the cause: filthy conversation. The rescue displays God delivering the righteous from the wicked. The verse displays the Lot: just. The verse displays the vexing: by the wicked. The verse displays the deliverance: granted. The deliverance of righteous Lot, distressed by the wicked, displays the counterpart to the judgment on Sodom: God delivered righteous Lot, whose soul was distressed by the filthy conduct of the wicked around him — showing that God rescues the godly even amid the doomed. The verse displays the rescued Lot, his righteous soul daily tormented (v.8).
8:
"(For that righteous man dwelling among them, in seeing and hearing, vexed his righteous soul from day to day with their unlawful deeds;)" The brief verse notes Lot's righteous soul daily tormented by the unlawful deeds around him. The grammatical voices note the inward distress. Barnes and Robertson note the explanation of Lot's vexation: "for that righteous man dwelling among them, in seeing and hearing, vexed (tormented, ebasanizen) his righteous soul from day to day with their unlawful (lawless) deeds" — living among the wicked, Lot was daily tormented in his righteous soul by what he saw and heard of their lawless conduct.

On "in seeing and hearing," the witnesses note the constant exposure. Barnes and Calvin note that Lot's torment came "in seeing and hearing" — the unavoidable daily exposure to the wickedness of Sodom; he could not escape the sights and sounds of their sin, and they pained him continually.

On "vexed his righteous soul from day to day," the puritan Henry notes the daily anguish: his "righteous soul" was vexed "from day to day" — a continual, daily torment; the righteous man, unable to share or approve the surrounding sin, suffered inwardly because of it; the mark of a godly soul is grief over the sin it must witness. The verse displays Lot's righteous soul daily tormented by the unlawful deeds around him.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the inward distress — "that righteous man dwelling among them... vexed (tormented) his righteous soul from day to day with their unlawful deeds," Lot daily tormented by the lawless conduct around him (Barnes, Robertson); "in seeing and hearing," the unavoidable daily exposure to Sodom's wickedness (Barnes, Calvin); "vexed his righteous soul from day to day," continual daily anguish, the godly grieved by witnessed sin (Henry). The verse displays the tormented soul. The verse marks the dwelling: among the wicked. The verse displays the exposure: seeing and hearing. The verse displays the torment: his righteous soul. The verse displays the duration: day to day. The distress displays the righteous soul pained by surrounding sin. The verse displays the righteous: tormented. The verse displays the deeds: unlawful. The verse displays the soul: vexed. Lot's righteous soul daily tormented by the unlawful deeds around him displays the inward suffering of the godly amid the wicked: dwelling in Sodom, the righteous Lot was daily tormented in his soul by all he saw and heard of their lawless deeds. The verse displays the daily-vexed soul, the ground of God's knowing how to deliver the godly (v.9).
9:
"The Lord knoweth how to deliver the godly out of temptations, and to reserve the unjust unto the day of judgment to be punished:" The witnesses note that the Lord delivers the godly and reserves the unjust for judgment. On "the Lord knoweth how to deliver the godly out of temptations," the grammatical voices note the conclusion. Barnes and Robertson note the lesson drawn from the three examples: "the Lord knoweth how (has the wisdom and power) to deliver the godly out of temptations (trials, testings)" — the examples of Noah and Lot prove that God knows how to rescue the godly from amid trials and the surrounding wicked; deliverance is assured for the righteous.

On "deliver the godly out of temptations," the witnesses note the comfort. Barnes and Calvin note the encouragement: God is able to rescue his people even from the midst of the most corrupt and dangerous surroundings; the godly need not fear, for God knows how to preserve them.

On "to reserve the unjust unto the day of judgment to be punished," the puritan Henry notes the other side: God also knows how "to reserve (keep) the unjust unto the day of judgment to be punished" — the wicked are kept in custody, reserved for punishment at the final judgment; the same God who delivers the godly holds the ungodly for their doom. The verse displays that the Lord delivers the godly and reserves the unjust for judgment.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the conclusion — "the Lord knoweth how to deliver the godly out of temptations (trials)," God able to rescue the godly from trials and the wicked, proved by Noah and Lot (Barnes, Robertson); the comfort that God preserves his people amid corruption (Barnes, Calvin); "to reserve the unjust unto the day of judgment to be punished," the wicked held for punishment at the final judgment (Henry). The verse displays the double lesson. The verse marks the deliverance: of the godly. The verse displays the temptations: escaped. The verse displays the reservation: of the unjust. The verse displays the judgment: to come. The conclusion displays God rescuing the godly and holding the wicked for doom. The verse displays the godly: delivered. The verse displays the unjust: reserved. The verse displays the judgment: appointed. That the Lord delivers the godly and reserves the unjust for judgment displays the comforting and solemn conclusion of the three examples: God knows how to rescue the godly out of their trials, and to keep the unjust under custody for punishment at the day of judgment. The verse displays the discriminating judgment, falling especially on the presumptuous (v.10).
10:
"But chiefly them that walk after the flesh in the lust of uncleanness, and despise government. Presumptuous are they, selfwilled, they are not afraid to speak evil of dignities." The witnesses note the chief offenders: the sensual and the despisers of authority. On "them that walk after the flesh in the lust of uncleanness, and despise government," the grammatical voices note the twofold sin. Barnes and Robertson note the description of those reserved especially for judgment: "chiefly them that walk after the flesh in the lust of uncleanness (defiling, sensual desire), and despise government (kyriotetos — lordship, authority, dominion)" — the false teachers are marked above all by sensuality and by contempt for authority; fleshly lust and rebellion against rule.

On "despise government," the witnesses note the rebellion. Barnes and Calvin note that they "despise government (authority, dominion)" — they reject and scorn all rightful authority, whether civil, ecclesiastical, or divine; lawlessness is their character.

On "presumptuous are they, selfwilled, they are not afraid to speak evil of dignities," the puritan Henry notes their arrogance: "presumptuous (daring, audacious) are they, selfwilled (arrogant, self-pleasing), they are not afraid to speak evil of (blaspheme, revile) dignities (glories, exalted beings or authorities)" — their boldness goes so far as to revile dignities, the exalted ones (whether angelic powers or earthly authorities); their arrogance knows no reverence. The verse displays the chief offenders: the sensual and the despisers of authority.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the twofold sin — "them that walk after the flesh in the lust of uncleanness, and despise government (authority)," marked by sensuality and contempt for authority (Barnes, Robertson); "despise government," scorning all rightful authority, lawless (Barnes, Calvin); "presumptuous, selfwilled, not afraid to speak evil of dignities," their arrogance reviling exalted beings or authorities (Henry). The verse displays the chief offenders. The verse marks the flesh: lust of uncleanness. The verse displays the contempt: despise government. The verse displays the arrogance: presumptuous, selfwilled. The verse displays the blasphemy: of dignities. The description displays the sensual, lawless, arrogant false teachers. The verse displays the flesh: followed. The verse displays the government: despised. The verse displays the dignities: reviled. The chief offenders — the sensual and the despisers of authority — display those reserved especially for judgment: those who walk after the flesh in defiling lust and despise authority, presumptuous and self-willed, not even afraid to revile dignities. The verse displays the arrogant revilers, surpassed in restraint by the angels (v.11).
11:
"Whereas angels, which are greater in power and might, bring not railing accusation against them before the Lord." The brief verse notes that even angels do not rail against dignities. The grammatical voices note the contrast of restraint. Barnes and Robertson note the comparison shaming the false teachers: "whereas angels, which are greater (than these false teachers) in power and might, bring not railing accusation (a reviling, blasphemous judgment) against them (the dignities, or perhaps the false teachers themselves) before the Lord" — even the holy angels, far greater than the arrogant false teachers, do not bring reviling charges; their superior power is matched by reverent restraint.

On "greater in power and might," the witnesses note the angels' superiority. Barnes and Calvin note that the angels are vastly "greater in power and might" than these presumptuous men; yet, despite their greatness, they refrain from the railing the false teachers freely indulge.

On "bring not railing accusation... before the Lord," the grammatical-historical note the reverence (cf. Jude 9): the angels "bring not railing accusation before the Lord" — they leave judgment to God, not presuming to revile; their restraint before the Lord rebukes the reckless blasphemy of the false teachers, who dare what even angels will not. The verse displays that even angels do not rail against dignities.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the contrast — "angels, which are greater in power and might, bring not railing accusation against them before the Lord," even the holy angels refraining from reviling charges (Barnes, Robertson); the angels vastly greater yet restrained, unlike the presumptuous men (Barnes, Calvin); the angels leaving judgment to God (cf. Jude 9), rebuking the false teachers' blasphemy (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the angelic restraint. The verse marks the angels: greater in power. The verse displays the restraint: no railing. The verse displays the place: before the Lord. The verse displays the rebuke: of the false teachers. The contrast displays the angels' reverence shaming the revilers. The verse displays the angels: powerful. The verse displays the accusation: withheld. The verse displays the reverence: kept. That even angels do not rail against dignities displays the rebuke of the false teachers' arrogance: the holy angels, far greater in power and might, do not bring reviling accusations before the Lord — leaving judgment to God; how reckless, then, is the blasphemy of these men who dare what even angels will not. The verse displays the restrained angels, against the brute-like railing of the false teachers (v.12).
12:
"But these, as natural brute beasts, made to be taken and destroyed, speak evil of the things that they understand not; and shall utterly perish in their own corruption;" The witnesses note the false teachers like brute beasts, perishing in their corruption. On "these, as natural brute beasts, made to be taken and destroyed," the grammatical voices note the comparison. Barnes and Robertson note the degrading likeness: "these, as natural brute beasts (irrational animals, governed by instinct), made (born) to be taken (captured) and destroyed" — the false teachers are like unreasoning animals, creatures of mere instinct, born only to be caught and killed; they live by appetite, not reason.

On "speak evil of the things that they understand not," the witnesses note their ignorant blasphemy. Barnes and Calvin note that they "speak evil of (blaspheme, revile) the things that they understand not" — they revile what they do not comprehend; their blasphemy springs from ignorance, railing against truths beyond their grasp.

On "shall utterly perish in their own corruption," the grammatical-historical note the doom: they "shall utterly perish (be destroyed) in their own corruption (destruction)" — like the beasts to which they are likened, they will perish; their own corruption is the ruin that destroys them; the doom fits the brutish life. The verse displays the false teachers like brute beasts, perishing in their corruption.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the likeness — "these, as natural brute beasts (irrational animals), made to be taken and destroyed," the false teachers like unreasoning animals, born to be caught and killed (Barnes, Robertson); "speak evil of the things that they understand not," reviling what they do not comprehend, blasphemy from ignorance (Barnes, Calvin); "shall utterly perish in their own corruption," perishing like the beasts, ruined by their own corruption (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the brutish doom. The verse marks the likeness: brute beasts. The verse displays the destiny: taken and destroyed. The verse displays the blasphemy: of things unknown. The verse displays the doom: perish in corruption. The comparison displays the false teachers as brutes destined for ruin. The verse displays the beasts: irrational. The verse displays the blasphemy: ignorant. The verse displays the perishing: certain. The false teachers like brute beasts, perishing in their corruption, display the degrading verdict on them: like irrational animals born to be caught and destroyed, they revile what they do not understand and will utterly perish in their own corruption. The verse displays the perishing brutes, whose riotous feasting is their shame (v.13).
13:
"And shall receive the reward of unrighteousness, as they that count it pleasure to riot in the day time. Spots they are and blemishes, sporting themselves with their own deceivings while they feast with you;" The witnesses note the false teachers as spots and blemishes, reveling shamelessly. On "shall receive the reward of unrighteousness, as they that count it pleasure to riot in the day time," the grammatical voices note their shameless excess. Barnes and Robertson note their character: "shall receive the reward (wages) of unrighteousness, as they that count it pleasure to riot (revel, indulge in luxury) in the day time" — they will be paid the wages of their unrighteousness; they are people who delight in daytime revelry, openly indulging in dissipation without even the cover of night.

On "spots they are and blemishes," the witnesses note their defilement. Barnes and Calvin note that "spots (stains) they are and blemishes (disgraces)" — the false teachers are blots and disgraces on the Christian community; far from adorning the church, they defile and dishonour it.

On "sporting themselves with their own deceivings while they feast with you," the puritan Henry notes their revelry at the love-feasts: "sporting themselves (reveling) with their own deceivings (deceptions, or luxuries) while they feast with you" — they revel in their deceptions even as they share the believers' feasts (the agape love-feasts), turning sacred fellowship into self-indulgence; their presence corrupts the very feasts of charity. The verse displays the false teachers as spots and blemishes, reveling shamelessly.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the shameless excess — "shall receive the reward of unrighteousness, as they that count it pleasure to riot in the day time," paid the wages of unrighteousness, delighting in open daytime revelry (Barnes, Robertson); "spots they are and blemishes," blots and disgraces defiling the church (Barnes, Calvin); "sporting themselves with their own deceivings while they feast with you," reveling at the love-feasts, corrupting sacred fellowship (Henry). The verse displays the defiling revelers. The verse marks the reward: of unrighteousness. The verse displays the riot: in daytime. The verse displays the defilement: spots and blemishes. The verse displays the revelry: at the feasts. The description displays the false teachers defiling the fellowship. The verse displays the reward: wages of sin. The verse displays the spots: defiling. The verse displays the feasting: corrupted. The false teachers as spots and blemishes, reveling shamelessly, display their disgraceful character: they will receive the wages of unrighteousness, delighting in open revelry, blots and disgraces who revel in their deceptions even while feasting among the believers. The verse displays the defiling revelers, with eyes full of adultery (v.14).
14:
"Having eyes full of adultery, and that cannot cease from sin; beguiling unstable souls: an heart they have exercised with covetous practices; cursed children:" The witnesses note the false teachers full of lust and greed, beguiling the unstable. On "having eyes full of adultery, and that cannot cease from sin," the grammatical voices note their insatiable lust. Barnes and Robertson note the description: "having eyes full of adultery (literally, full of an adulteress — eyes that see every woman as an object of lust), and that cannot cease from sin (unable to stop sinning, never satisfied)" — their very eyes are saturated with lust, and they are incapable of ceasing from sin; sin has become their settled, unbreakable habit.

On "beguiling unstable souls," the witnesses note their seduction. Barnes and Calvin note that they go about "beguiling (enticing, luring, baiting) unstable (ungrounded, unsteady) souls" — they prey on those not firmly grounded in the faith, luring the weak and unstable into their errors and vices.

On "an heart they have exercised with covetous practices; cursed children," the puritan Henry notes their trained greed and their doom: "an heart they have exercised (trained, disciplined) with covetous practices (greed)" — their hearts are practiced experts in covetousness; and they are "cursed children (children of the curse)" — under God's curse, destined for condemnation. The verse displays the false teachers full of lust and greed, beguiling the unstable.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the insatiable lust — "having eyes full of adultery, and that cannot cease from sin," eyes saturated with lust, unable to stop sinning (Barnes, Robertson); "beguiling unstable souls," luring the ungrounded and weak into error (Barnes, Calvin); "an heart... exercised with covetous practices; cursed children," hearts trained in greed, under God's curse (Henry). The verse displays the lustful, greedy seducers. The verse marks the eyes: full of adultery. The verse displays the sinning: ceaseless. The verse displays the seduction: of unstable souls. The verse displays the heart: trained in greed. The verse displays the doom: cursed children. The description displays the false teachers as practiced sinners under the curse. The verse displays the lust: insatiable. The verse displays the greed: practiced. The verse displays the curse: upon them. The false teachers full of lust and greed, beguiling the unstable, display the depth of their corruption: with eyes full of adultery and unable to cease from sin, they lure unstable souls; their hearts are trained in covetousness, and they are children of the curse. The verse displays the cursed seducers, who have forsaken the right way (v.15).
15:
"Which have forsaken the right way, and are gone astray, following the way of Balaam the son of Bosor, who loved the wages of unrighteousness;" The witnesses note the false teachers following the greedy way of Balaam. On "which have forsaken the right way, and are gone astray," the grammatical voices note the apostasy. Barnes and Robertson note the departure: "which have forsaken (abandoned) the right (straight) way, and are gone astray (wandered off)" — the false teachers have abandoned the straight path of truth and righteousness and wandered into error; deliberate departure from the right way.

On "following the way of Balaam the son of Bosor," the witnesses note the type. Barnes and Calvin note the comparison: "following the way of Balaam the son of Bosor (Beor)" — they imitate Balaam, the Old Testament prophet who, for reward, sought to curse Israel and led them into sin (Numbers 22-24); Balaam is the type of the religious teacher corrupted by greed.

On "who loved the wages of unrighteousness," the puritan Henry notes Balaam's greed: Balaam "loved the wages (reward) of unrighteousness" — he loved the money offered him for wrongdoing; greed for gain was his ruin, as it is the false teachers'; the love of ill-gotten reward marks them both. The verse displays the false teachers following the greedy way of Balaam.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the apostasy — "have forsaken the right way, and are gone astray," abandoning the straight path of truth and wandering into error (Barnes, Robertson); "following the way of Balaam the son of Bosor," imitating Balaam, the prophet corrupted by greed (Numbers 22-24, Barnes, Calvin); "who loved the wages of unrighteousness," Balaam's love of ill-gotten reward, the mark of the false teachers (Henry). The verse displays the way of Balaam. The verse marks the forsaking: the right way. The verse displays the straying: into error. The verse displays the type: Balaam. The verse displays the greed: wages of unrighteousness. The comparison displays the false teachers as Balaams loving gain. The verse displays the way: forsaken. The verse displays the Balaam: followed. The verse displays the wages: loved. The false teachers following the greedy way of Balaam display the type of their corruption: having forsaken the right way and gone astray, they follow Balaam, who loved the wages of unrighteousness — greed for gain their common ruin. The verse displays the way of Balaam, rebuked by the dumb donkey (v.16).
16:
"But was rebuked for his iniquity: the dumb ass speaking with man's voice forbad the madness of the prophet." The brief verse notes Balaam rebuked by the dumb donkey for his madness. The grammatical voices note the rebuke. Barnes and Robertson note the account (Numbers 22): "but was rebuked (reproved) for his iniquity (transgression): the dumb ass (donkey) speaking with man's voice forbad (restrained, hindered) the madness of the prophet" — Balaam was rebuked for his sin by his own donkey, which God enabled to speak with a human voice, checking the prophet's mad course.

On "the dumb ass speaking with man's voice," the witnesses note the miracle. Barnes and Calvin note the wonder: God made the speechless animal speak — a striking rebuke, that a dumb beast should reprove the prophet; the lower creature corrected the higher, shaming Balaam's folly.

On "forbad the madness of the prophet," the grammatical-historical note the folly named: the donkey "forbad (restrained) the madness (paraphronian — insane folly) of the prophet" — Balaam's covetous course is called "madness," an irrational folly; that it took a speaking donkey to check him underscores how far greed had blinded him. The verse displays Balaam rebuked by the dumb donkey for his madness.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the rebuke — "was rebuked for his iniquity: the dumb ass speaking with man's voice forbad the madness of the prophet," Balaam rebuked for his sin by his own donkey, divinely enabled to speak (Numbers 22, Barnes, Robertson); the miracle of the speechless animal speaking, shaming the prophet (Barnes, Calvin); "the madness of the prophet," Balaam's covetous course an irrational folly checked by the donkey (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the rebuke. The verse marks the rebuke: for iniquity. The verse displays the donkey: speaking. The verse displays the voice: man's. The verse displays the folly: the prophet's madness. The rebuke displays the dumb beast checking the mad prophet. The verse displays the iniquity: reproved. The verse displays the ass: speaking. The verse displays the madness: forbidden. Balaam rebuked by the dumb donkey for his madness displays the shame of his greed: he was reproved for his sin when his own donkey, speaking with a human voice, restrained the madness of the prophet — a dumb beast rebuking the covetous folly that blinded him. The verse displays the rebuked prophet, a warning embodied in the waterless wells (v.17).
17:
"These are wells without water, clouds that are carried with a tempest; to whom the mist of darkness is reserved for ever." The witnesses note the false teachers as empty wells and waterless clouds, reserved for darkness. On "these are wells without water, clouds that are carried with a tempest," the grammatical voices note the figures of emptiness. Barnes and Robertson note the double image: "these are wells (springs) without water, clouds that are carried (driven) with a tempest (storm, whirlwind)" — the false teachers promise much but give nothing, like a spring that has no water or a cloud that brings no rain but is driven off by the storm; empty and disappointing.

On "wells without water," the witnesses note the false promise. Barnes and Calvin note that a "well without water" deceives the thirsty traveler who comes expecting refreshment and finds none; so the false teachers promise spiritual truth and life but yield nothing; their show is empty.

On "to whom the mist of darkness is reserved for ever," the puritan Henry notes the doom: "to whom the mist (blackness, gloom) of darkness is reserved (kept) for ever" — the blackest darkness is reserved for them eternally; their empty deception ends in everlasting gloom; the doom of the deceivers. The verse displays the false teachers as empty wells and waterless clouds, reserved for darkness.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the figures — "wells without water, clouds that are carried with a tempest," the false teachers promising much but giving nothing, empty and disappointing (Barnes, Robertson); a "well without water" deceiving the thirsty, the show empty (Barnes, Calvin); "the mist of darkness is reserved for ever," the blackest darkness reserved eternally, the doom of the deceivers (Henry). The verse displays the empty deceivers. The verse marks the wells: without water. The verse displays the clouds: storm-driven. The verse displays the emptiness: no refreshment. The verse displays the doom: darkness for ever. The figures display the false teachers empty and doomed to gloom. The verse displays the wells: dry. The verse displays the clouds: rainless. The verse displays the darkness: reserved. The false teachers as empty wells and waterless clouds, reserved for darkness, display their emptiness and doom: they are springs without water and clouds driven by the storm, promising refreshment but yielding none — and the blackest darkness is reserved for them forever. The verse displays the doomed deceivers, who entice with empty words (v.18).
18:
"For when they speak great swelling words of vanity, they allure through the lusts of the flesh, through much wantonness, those that were clean escaped from them who live in error." The witnesses note the false teachers luring back through fleshly lusts those newly escaped. On "when they speak great swelling words of vanity," the grammatical voices note the empty bombast. Barnes and Robertson note their inflated speech: "when they speak great swelling (huperonka — bombastic, pompous, overblown) words of vanity (emptiness)" — the false teachers utter pompous, high-sounding but empty words; impressive in form, hollow in substance.

On "they allure through the lusts of the flesh, through much wantonness," the witnesses note the bait. Barnes and Calvin note the method: "they allure (entice, bait) through the lusts of the flesh, through much wantonness (lasciviousness)" — they lure their victims by appealing to fleshly desires, baiting them with sensual indulgence; the bait is the gratification of lust.

On "those that were clean escaped from them who live in error," the puritan Henry notes the targets. Barnes and Calvin note that they target "those that were clean (just now, barely) escaped from them who live in error" — recent converts, who had just escaped from the pagan life of error, are the prey; the false teachers seek to lure back into corruption those newly delivered from it. The verse displays the false teachers luring back through fleshly lusts those newly escaped.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the bombast — "when they speak great swelling (pompous) words of vanity (emptiness)," pompous but hollow speech (Barnes, Robertson); "allure through the lusts of the flesh, through much wantonness," baiting victims with sensual indulgence (Barnes, Calvin); "those that were clean escaped from them who live in error," targeting recent converts just delivered from the pagan life (Henry). The verse displays the luring. The verse marks the words: great swelling, vain. The verse displays the bait: lusts of the flesh. The verse displays the method: wantonness. The verse displays the prey: the newly escaped. The luring displays the false teachers baiting young converts back into sin. The verse displays the words: empty. The verse displays the allure: fleshly. The verse displays the escaped: targeted. The false teachers luring back through fleshly lusts those newly escaped display their seductive method: with pompous, empty words they entice, through the lusts of the flesh and much wantonness, those who had just escaped from the life of error — seeking to draw the new convert back into corruption. The verse displays the luring deceivers, promising a liberty that is bondage (v.19).
19:
"While they promise them liberty, they themselves are the servants of corruption: for of whom a man is overcome, of the same is he brought in bondage." The witnesses note the false teachers promising liberty while themselves slaves of corruption. On "while they promise them liberty, they themselves are the servants of corruption," the grammatical voices note the hollow promise. Barnes and Robertson note the irony: "while they promise them liberty (freedom), they themselves are the servants (slaves) of corruption (moral depravity)" — the false teachers offer freedom (likely freedom from moral restraint), yet they are themselves enslaved to corruption; they cannot give what they do not have.

On "they themselves are the servants of corruption," the witnesses note their bondage. Barnes and Calvin note the contradiction: those who promise liberty are themselves slaves — bound by their own lusts and corruption; their "freedom" is the worst slavery, the bondage of sin.

On "of whom a man is overcome, of the same is he brought in bondage," the puritan Henry notes the principle: "of whom (whatever) a man is overcome (mastered, conquered), of the same is he brought in bondage (enslaved)" — whatever masters a man enslaves him; the one conquered by his lusts is the slave of his lusts; the false teachers, overcome by corruption, are its bondservants, and they would enslave others under the name of freedom. The verse displays the false teachers promising liberty while themselves slaves of corruption.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the irony — "while they promise them liberty, they themselves are the servants (slaves) of corruption," offering freedom while enslaved to corruption (Barnes, Robertson); those promising liberty themselves bound by their lusts, their "freedom" the worst slavery (Barnes, Calvin); "of whom a man is overcome, of the same is he brought in bondage," whatever masters a man enslaves him (Henry). The verse displays the enslaved promisers. The verse marks the promise: liberty. The verse displays the reality: slaves of corruption. The verse displays the principle: the conquered enslaved. The verse displays the contradiction: bound, yet promising freedom. The irony displays the false teachers enslaved by what they call liberty. The verse displays the liberty: false. The verse displays the bondage: real. The verse displays the corruption: their master. The false teachers promising liberty while themselves slaves of corruption display the hollowness of their offer: they promise freedom, yet are themselves enslaved to corruption — for whatever masters a man enslaves him; their "liberty" is the bondage of sin. The verse displays the enslaving deceivers, whose dupes end worse than they began (v.20).
20:
"For if after they have escaped the pollutions of the world through the knowledge of the Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ, they are again entangled therein, and overcome, the latter end is worse with them than the beginning." The witnesses note the worse end of those who relapse after escaping. On "if after they have escaped the pollutions of the world through the knowledge of the Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ," the grammatical voices note the escape. Barnes and Robertson note the case described: "if after they have escaped (fled from) the pollutions (defilements) of the world through the knowledge (epignosis) of the Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ" — these had once escaped the world's corruption through the knowledge of Christ; an apparent conversion, a real outward reformation.

On "they are again entangled therein, and overcome," the witnesses note the relapse. Barnes and Calvin note the tragic return: "they are again entangled therein (in the pollutions), and overcome (mastered)" — having escaped, they are again caught and conquered by the corruption they had left; the relapse into the old sins.

On "the latter end is worse with them than the beginning," the puritan Henry notes the aggravated doom: "the latter end is worse with them than the beginning" — their final state is worse than their first; the relapse after knowledge is more guilty and more ruinous than the original heathen state; to have known and forsaken the way is worse than never to have known it (cf. Matthew 12:45). The verse displays the worse end of those who relapse after escaping.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the escape — "after they have escaped the pollutions of the world through the knowledge of the Lord and Saviour Jesus Christ," an outward escape from corruption through the knowledge of Christ (Barnes, Robertson); "again entangled therein, and overcome," the tragic relapse, caught again by the old corruption (Barnes, Calvin); "the latter end is worse... than the beginning," the final state worse than the first, the relapse more guilty than the original state (Matthew 12:45, Henry). The verse displays the worse relapse. The verse marks the escape: from pollutions. The verse displays the means: knowledge of Christ. The verse displays the relapse: entangled, overcome. The verse displays the doom: worse than the beginning. The case displays the aggravated ruin of those who relapse. The verse displays the escaped: entangled again. The verse displays the end: worse. The verse displays the knowledge: forsaken. The worse end of those who relapse after escaping displays the solemn warning: those who had escaped the world's pollutions through the knowledge of Christ, if again entangled and overcome, are in a worse state at the last than at the first — the relapse more ruinous than the original corruption. The verse displays the worse end, illustrated by two proverbs (vv.21-22).
21:
"For it had been better for them not to have known the way of righteousness, than, after they have known it, to turn from the holy commandment delivered unto them." The brief verse notes that it were better never to have known the way than to turn from it. The grammatical voices note the aggravated guilt of apostasy. Barnes and Robertson note the principle: "it had been better for them not to have known the way of righteousness, than, after they have known it, to turn (turn back) from the holy commandment delivered unto them" — to know the way of righteousness and then forsake it is worse than never to have known it; knowledge brings greater responsibility and greater guilt in apostasy.

On "not to have known the way of righteousness," the witnesses note the responsibility of knowledge. Barnes and Calvin note that knowledge of "the way of righteousness" (the gospel, the Christian path) increases accountability; the one who knew and turned away sins against light, more guilty than the ignorant.

On "to turn from the holy commandment delivered unto them," the puritan Henry notes the apostasy: to "turn from the holy commandment (the gospel rule of life) delivered unto them" is deliberate apostasy — a turning away from the known truth and rule; this rejection of received light is the height of their guilt. The verse displays that it were better never to have known the way than to turn from it.

The very brief comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the principle — "better for them not to have known the way of righteousness, than... to turn from the holy commandment," knowing and forsaking the way worse than never knowing it (Barnes, Robertson); knowledge of the way increasing accountability, sinning against light more guilty (Barnes, Calvin); "turn from the holy commandment delivered unto them," deliberate apostasy from known truth (Henry). The verse displays the aggravated guilt. The verse marks the knowing: the way of righteousness. The verse displays the better: never to have known. The verse displays the turning: from the commandment. The verse displays the guilt: against light. The principle displays apostasy from known truth as the worse sin. The verse displays the way: known. The verse displays the commandment: forsaken. The verse displays the guilt: aggravated. That it were better never to have known the way than to turn from it displays the height of the apostates' guilt: it would have been better never to have known the way of righteousness than, having known it, to turn away from the holy commandment delivered to them — for to sin against received light is the worse condemnation. The verse displays the aggravated apostasy, fixed by two homely proverbs (v.22).
22:
"But it is happened unto them according to the true proverb, The dog is turned to his own vomit again; and the sow that was washed to her wallowing in the mire." The witnesses note the two proverbs picturing the relapse into corruption. On "the true proverb, The dog is turned to his own vomit again," the grammatical voices note the first proverb. Barnes and Robertson note the figure (Proverbs 26:11): "the true proverb, The dog is turned to (returns to) his own vomit again" — the relapsed apostate is like a dog returning to its vomit; what it had cast off it takes up again; the disgusting picture of returning to forsaken sin.

On "the dog... his own vomit," the witnesses note the unchanged nature. Barnes and Calvin note that the dog returns to its vomit because its nature is unchanged; the proverb exposes that the apostate's outward reformation never changed the inward nature; the dog was always a dog.

On "the sow that was washed to her wallowing in the mire," the grammatical-historical note the second proverb: "the sow that was washed (cleaned outwardly) to her wallowing in the mire" — the washed sow returns to the mud because, though washed outwardly, its nature loves the mire; so the apostate, outwardly cleansed but never inwardly renewed, returns to the filth of sin; both proverbs teach that the relapse reveals an unchanged heart. The verse displays the two proverbs picturing the relapse into corruption.

The thin comment matches the verse. The witnesses agree: the first proverb (Proverbs 26:11) — "The dog is turned to his own vomit again," the apostate like a dog returning to its vomit, taking up forsaken sin (Barnes, Robertson); the dog returning because its nature is unchanged, the outward reformation never changing the heart (Barnes, Calvin); "the sow that was washed to her wallowing in the mire," the washed sow returning to the mud it loves, outwardly cleansed but never inwardly renewed (grammatical-historical). The verse displays the relapse proverbs. The verse marks the dog: to its vomit. The verse displays the sow: to the mire. The verse displays the nature: unchanged. The verse displays the lesson: outward washing, no inward renewal. The proverbs display the relapse revealing an unchanged heart. The verse displays the vomit: returned to. The verse displays the mire: wallowed in. The verse displays the nature: unrenewed. The two proverbs picturing the relapse into corruption display the fitting close of the chapter on the false teachers: the relapsed apostate is like the dog that returns to its vomit and the washed sow that returns to the mire — outwardly cleansed but never inwardly renewed, their return to sin revealing a heart that was never truly changed. The verse displays the unchanged nature, closing the chapter on the false teachers and their doom.
